coaching.coaching: Pin down observable artifact proving context irrelevance with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: observable artifact proving context irrelevance

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind AC4 relevance-exception triggering condition first.
coaching.subject: AC4 relevance-exception triggering condition

coaching.coaching: Pin down PlanContext hierarchy-completeness schema field with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: PlanContext hierarchy-completeness schema field

coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] cited evidence line numbers in orchestrator will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: cited evidence line numbers in orchestrator

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for recoverability classification for local git-object reads.
coaching.subject: recoverability classification for local git-object reads

coaching.coaching: Pin down retry protocol parameters for child-list and fetch failures with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: retry protocol parameters for child-list and fetch failures

coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance-criteria verifiability and definition of done will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance-criteria verifiability and definition of done

coaching.coaching: Research prior art / OSS for prior art for relevance-exemption mechanisms before building it custom.
coaching.subject: prior art for relevance-exemption mechanisms

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'deterministic, tested proof' with a concrete observable artifact (e.g., 'a `@irrelevant_criteria` decorator on the criterion class, with a corresponding pytest fixture that asserts the omitted child has no fields referenced by the decorated criteria').
evidence: 'deterministic, tested proof' does not name the artifact: is it a code annotation, a metadata flag on the criterion, a separate test fixture, a static-analysis rule?
ABSENCE: No observable output is described — 'proof' is effort-framed rather than outcome-framed.
An executor implementing this exception has no specification of what 'done' looks like for this path.
findings.finding: AC4's 'deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant' is effort-framed and does not specify what observable artifact or mechanism constitutes such a proof, making it unevaluable in-session.
findings.location: AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'
scenarios: An agent implementing the relevance exception cannot know whether a boolean flag, a docstring, or a dedicated test class satisfies this criterion.
The AC cannot be checked in-session without knowing what artifact to inspect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4 to require that the relevance proof covers both the criteria that ran AND the criteria that were suppressed by the routing change caused by the incomplete hierarchy (i.e. the proof must account for `would-have-run` criteria, not only `did-run` criteria).
evidence: PlanContext.has_children returns bool(self.children) — an empty list from a failed enumeration makes a container appear as a leaf
orchestrator.py route_criteria and pass1.py line 347 branch on ctx.has_children to skip container criteria

If container criteria (P5, G3, G4, decomp-shape) never run because has_children is False, no 'currently running' criterion depends on children — AC4's condition is trivially met without any real relevance analysis
AC4 says 'irrelevant to every criterion being decided' — it does not say 'irrelevant to every criterion that would have been decided had the hierarchy been complete'
findings.finding: The AC4 relevance-exception can be satisfied vacuously because a failed child enumeration flips `has_children` to False, which changes routing and suppresses all container-specific criteria (P5, G3, G4, decomp-shape) — so 'every criterion being decided' trivially excludes the criteria that SHOULD have run. The plan requires a proof that omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided, but does not require the proof to account for criteria that were never dispatched due to the routing change caused by the incomplete hierarchy.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'
scenarios: A container ticket whose child enumeration fails silently: has_children=False, all container criteria skip, AC4 exception is met vacuously, ordinary PASS is returned despite the entire container review being unconducted
A relevance-exception implementation checks only the criteria that actually ran (a leaf set) and finds none depend on children — passes AC4 without ever gating the real problem
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC4 to: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion that would have been evaluated had the hierarchy been complete (including criteria suppressed by routing changes caused by the incomplete data).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the incomplete-hierarchy INDETERMINATE outcome reuses the existing `coverage.llm_unavailable` / `finalize_verdict` INDETERMINATE pathway or introduces a new coverage flag (e.g. `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete`), and list which downstream consumers (claim gate, attestation signing, review_bot, opcert_service) require updates for the new flag.
evidence: AC3: 'A remaining incomplete hierarchy cannot receive an ordinary clean pass.'
AC5: 'Explicit incomplete-context/indeterminate outcomes identify which hierarchy reads failed or were omitted.'
Existing prior art: `elif coverage.get('llm_unavailable'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` in finalize_verdict; `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` sets `coverage.llm_unavailable=True`.
The plan does not mention llm_unavailable, finalize_verdict, or the existing INDETERMINATE paths at all.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new 'explicit incomplete-context/indeterminate' verdict outcome (AC3, AC5) without acknowledging the system's existing llm_unavailable → INDETERMINATE pathway in finalize_verdict and _degraded_plan_review_verdict. There is no specification of whether the new outcome reuses the established coverage.llm_unavailable flag and INDETERMINATE verdict, or introduces a parallel mechanism (e.g., coverage.hierarchy_incomplete). A parallel mechanism would create two divergent INDETERMINATE code paths with different coverage shapes, complicating attestation, the claim gate, and consumers.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria AC3, AC5 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py finalize_verdict (lines 503-580)
scenarios: Two distinct INDETERMINATE shapes (llm_unavailable vs. hierarchy_incomplete) reach the claim gate and the downstream consumer (review_bot, opcert_service) handles only one, silently treating the other as a clean result.
Attestation logic that checks coverage.llm_ran / coverage.llm_unavailable misses the new hierarchy_incomplete flag and signs an incomplete review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete: true` flag alongside (not replacing) the existing patterns, update `finalize_verdict` to treat `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete` the same as `llm_unavailable` for the INDETERMINATE branch, and confirm that all four downstream consumers already read that branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the coverage/PlanContext field(s) that record hierarchy completeness (e.g., `children_enumeration_ok: bool`, `children_detail_partial: list[str]`) and specify how `finalize_verdict` routes on them (e.g., treat as `llm_unavailable`-equivalent when incomplete).
evidence: AC1: 'Plan context records whether child enumeration and child detail loading completed successfully.' — PlanContext (det_floor.py:104-142) has no such field; no coverage key is named.
finalize_verdict routes on named keys 'llm_unavailable' and 'verify_failed' (orchestrator.py:527-537); an analogous key for hierarchy incompleteness is required by AC3 but never specified.
_assemble_context_uncached (orchestrator.py:163-211) catches child-list and per-child fetch errors silently, constructing PlanContext with no completeness flag.
ABSENCE: grep for 'children_complete', 'enumeration_failed', 'hierarchy_incomplete', 'incomplete_hierarchy' returns zero matches across the module.
findings.finding: The coverage/PlanContext schema field name for hierarchy-completeness status is never defined, yet the verdict protocol (finalize_verdict) must route on it to prevent a clean PASS when children are missing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 and AC3; orchestrator.py:188 (PlanContext construction), orchestrator.py:503-580 (finalize_verdict)
scenarios: Child-list read fails transiently → PlanContext.children=[] → has_children=False → container criteria (G3/G4) never route → verdict is PASS with no record that children were expected.
One child's full state is missing → G3/G4 evaluate against a summary stub → LLM findings are degraded without disclosure to the Pass-2 verifier.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `children_enumeration_ok: bool` and `children_detail_failures: list[str]` to `PlanContext` (or to the `coverage` dict populated in `_assemble_context_uncached`), and add a branch in `finalize_verdict` that sets verdict='INDETERMINATE' when `coverage.get('children_enumeration_ok') is False`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the precise condition under which the relevance exception applies (e.g., 'the active routing includes no container criteria (G3/G4/decomp-shape) AND the ticket's prior signed attestation recorded children_enumeration_ok=true with children_count=0'), or drop the exception entirely if no safe condition can be stated.
evidence: AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.' — no condition is given for what constitutes irrelevance (e.g., no container criteria in the active routing? ticket_type is leaf? children count was zero in a prior signed attestation?).
AC6: 'Tests cover ... the relevance exception if implemented.' — the conditional 'if implemented' acknowledges the exception may not be present, but no specification is given for when it applies.
ABSENCE: no 'relevance_proof', 'omitted_context_irrelevant', or equivalent field/function exists in the module.
findings.finding: The 'deterministic, tested proof that omitted context is irrelevant' (the relevance exception in AC4) is a decision branch whose triggering condition is never defined, making the exception and its test case (AC6) impossible to specify or implement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 and AC6
scenarios: An implementer adds the exception for 'ticket has no children in the prior attestation' but another implementer uses 'ticket_type==leaf'; the verdicts diverge on the same input.
A test for 'relevance exception' cannot be written without knowing the condition — AC6 becomes a vacuous test of an undefined path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence line-range citations: replace `orchestrator.py:8-25` with `orchestrator.py:173-188` (child-enumeration catch + fallback in `_assemble_context_uncached`) and `orchestrator.py:26-49` with `orchestrator.py:188-211` (PlanContext construction from the potentially-incomplete `children` list).
evidence: Plan cites: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:8-25 catches child enumeration and fetch errors'
Actual file lines 8-25: module docstring text only — no exception handling, no `listed = []` fallback
Plan cites: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:26-49 builds review context from the potentially incomplete child set'
Actual file lines 26-49: `from __future__ import annotations`, `import contextlib`, `import contextvars`, etc. — pure imports, no context building
Actual catch-and-continue logic is at lines 173-188; PlanContext construction is at lines 188-211
findings.finding: The Evidence section cites 'orchestrator.py:8-25' as the location of child-enumeration error handling, but reading that file shows lines 8-25 are the module docstring prose. The actual catch/fallback logic is at lines 173-188 (_assemble_context_uncached). Similarly, 'orchestrator.py:26-49' is cited as where review context is built from the incomplete child set, but those lines are stdlib imports; the actual PlanContext assembly is at lines 188-211.
findings.location: Evidence section — 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:8-25' and 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:26-49'
scenarios: An implementor following the plan diffs/reads lines 8-25 expecting to find error-handling code and finds the docstring instead — they cannot locate the code to modify without re-searching.
A reviewer auditing whether the remediation was applied checks the cited lines and finds no change there, falsely concluding the work was not done.
findings.suggested_fix:
In the Evidence section, update the two citations:
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:173-188` — `except Exception` blocks that substitute `listed = []` and fall back to the summary `c` dict
- `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:188-211` — `PlanContext(…, children=children, …)` construction from the potentially-incomplete child set

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC6 with AC4: if the relevance exception is a permitted path (AC4), its tests are mandatory — change 'the relevance exception if implemented' to 'the relevance exception (required if the exception path is shipped)'.
evidence: AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided'
AC6: 'Tests cover … and the relevance exception if implemented'
AC4 frames the exception as a real allowed path; AC6 treats it as optional — a verifier cannot simultaneously require and optionally test the same behavior
findings.finding: AC4 mandates that the relevance exception IS the only permitted exception ('The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof…'), making the tested proof a mandatory deliverable; AC6 makes testing that exception conditional ('the relevance exception if implemented'), implying it may not be implemented at all. This creates a contradiction: if the exception exists (AC4 says it can), AC6 should require its tests unconditionally.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 vs AC6
scenarios: Implementer ships the relevance exception (allowed by AC4) but skips its tests because AC6 says 'if implemented', leaving untested exception logic in production
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC6's trailing clause with: '…and the relevance-exception path (mandatory test coverage if the exception is shipped).' This preserves optionality on shipping the feature while making tests non-optional once it exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'recoverable' concretely (e.g., 'IOError / network-timeout; excludes PermissionError and malformed-response') so the retry gate is unambiguous and testable.
evidence: AC2 uses 'recoverable' but the plan never defines what makes a list/fetch failure recoverable vs fatal (e.g., network timeout vs permission denied vs malformed response).
The cascade flag mentions 'recoverable reads' without specifying criteria.
ABSENCE: No exception type, HTTP status code range, or error category is given.
findings.finding: The term 'recoverable' (distinguishing which failures get retries) is undefined — an executor cannot determine without investigation which error types qualify.
findings.location: AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.'
scenarios: An implementing agent must choose an arbitrary recovery heuristic, leading to either over-retry (permanent errors) or under-retry (transient errors).
Reviewers cannot verify AC2 without knowing which error classes are in scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC2: 'Recoverable = transient I/O failures (e.g., `OSError`, `asyncio.TimeoutError`); non-recoverable = permission, not-found, or parse errors which skip to incomplete-context result immediately.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the bounded retry parameters for child-list/fetch failures: retry count (e.g., up to 3 attempts), backoff strategy (e.g., exponential with cap), and whether retries apply at the list level, per-child level, or both.
evidence: AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.' — no retry count, no backoff policy, no placement (wrapper vs inline) is given.
Current code: orchestrator.py:173-187 has bare `except Exception: listed = []` and `except Exception: children.append(c)` with a single `logger.warning` — no retry logic.
ABSENCE: grep for 'retry' in plan_review/ returns only unrelated hits (resign.py relation failures, criteria_routing.json).
findings.finding: The retry protocol for recoverable child-list/fetch failures is referenced but its parameters (retry count, backoff, placement in the call stack) are never specified, leaving the AC unimplementable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2; orchestrator.py:173-187 (_assemble_context_uncached child enumeration)
scenarios: A transient 503 on the ticket store causes child enumeration to fail; with no retry the review silently proceeds with zero children, which under AC3 should produce INDETERMINATE.
Without a specified retry count an executor might implement 1 retry or 10, making behaviour non-deterministic across implementations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC2 a specification of what constitutes a 'recoverable' failure (e.g. transient I/O / network errors) versus a permanent one (e.g. not-found / permission-denied), so the retry boundary is unambiguous to the implementer.
evidence: orchestrator.py line 175: `except Exception:` catch-all for child enumeration — no distinction between transient and permanent errors
orchestrator.py line 186: `except Exception:` catch-all for per-child fetch — same issue
The plan uses the word 'recoverable' without defining the recovery predicate or error classification
findings.finding: AC2 requires 'recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries' but does not define what distinguishes a recoverable (transient) failure from a permanent one. The current code uses a bare `except Exception` catch-all; layering retries on it without a recoverability predicate would retry 404/missing-ticket errors indefinitely (or up to the bound), adding latency with no benefit, or would require implementers to make a non-trivial design decision the plan defers silently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.'
scenarios: A child ticket was deleted (404); the retry loop retries 3× with backoff, all fail, adding multi-second latency before reaching the same INDETERMINATE outcome
A network timeout on the ticket store is not retried because the implementer classifies it as permanent, silently defeating AC2's intent
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC2 to: 'Recoverable failures (transient I/O or network errors) receive bounded retries (≤N attempts, with exponential backoff); permanent failures (not-found, permission-denied) are classified immediately as incomplete without retry. Each failure path emits an actionable diagnostic identifying the specific read that failed and its error class.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_reads.list_tickets` / `_reads.show_ticket` actually exhibit transient failures (cite an incident or test case) before specifying bounded retries; if they don't, replace the retry AC with a 'log + immediate INDETERMINATE' AC that uses the existing verdict plumbing.
evidence: `_reads.show_ticket` / `_reads.list_tickets` delegate to `rebar._engine_support.reads` (local git-store reads via `ticket_reads.show_state` / `ticket_reads.list_states`) — not HTTP calls.
The existing retry infrastructure (`_build_retrying_anthropic_model` + tenacity, `_call_with_retry` in dispatch_one.py) is explicitly scoped to HTTP/network calls (Anthropic API, Jira REST).
Plan cites no incident log, transient-failure report, or scale estimate justifying transient-failure handling on local git reads.

Absence of any current retry on these reads across all existing call-sites (orchestrator.py:174, orchestrator.py:249, attest.py:783, completion.py:117, operations.py:95) — no evidence the three existing callers ever needed retry.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'bounded retries with actionable diagnostics' for `list_tickets` / `show_ticket` failures, but these reads are local git-object reads against a local orphan branch (not HTTP or network calls). No profiling result, incident report, or scale estimate in the plan establishes that transient failures occur here. The well-used retry pattern in this codebase (`_call_with_retry`, tenacity/httpx transport) is justified for HTTP/network calls; applying it to local filesystem/git reads cargo-cults that pattern without evidence of the failure mode it would address.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.'
scenarios: A retry loop on a git-object read that fails due to a corrupt store or a missing ticket will spin on a deterministic error, burning time without ever succeeding, while a simpler 'fail fast + INDETERMINATE' would be correct and already exists.
A git lock contention on the tickets branch already has its own backoff in gitutil.py (not exposed through _reads); double-retrying at the orchestrator layer would nest two retry envelopes.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC2 ('Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics') with: 'A child-list or child-fetch failure is immediately classified as an incomplete-context condition, logged with the exception, and routed to an INDETERMINATE verdict — no retry is performed on local reads unless a specific transient failure mode is first evidenced.' This reuses the finalize_verdict INDETERMINATE path already in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or defer AC4 (the relevance-exemption escape-hatch) unless a concrete current criterion and scenario that requires it can be named; the simpler fail-closed rule (incomplete context → always INDETERMINATE) satisfies the ticket's stated goal with no new abstraction.
evidence: No existing code in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ or adjacent modules implements or references a 'relevance proof' or 'omitted-context irrelevance' concept (grep confirms zero hits for 'relevance_exception', 'relevance_proof', 'omitted context', 'deterministic proof').
The existing INDETERMINATE posture is a blanket fail-closed rule with no per-criterion relevance carve-out anywhere in the codebase.

The plan's own remediation section says 'prohibit an ordinary clean pass unless the system can deterministically prove the missing context is irrelevant' — the 'unless' clause is purely hypothetical with no identified current scenario that would ever trigger it.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 ('The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided') introduces a novel relevance-exemption mechanism with zero existing call-sites in the plan-review codebase. It requires building a determinism/proof infrastructure for a hypothetical future escape-hatch that no current done-definition requires. This satisfies neither the Rule of Three (0 call-sites) nor YAGNI (no current AC requires the exemption to be exercised).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'
scenarios: Implementing the exemption path requires: (a) a relevance oracle per criterion, (b) a test harness for the proof, (c) ongoing maintenance as new criteria are added. All of this for a path that may never fire.
If the exemption is never triggered, its code is dead weight; if it is triggered incorrectly it would allow a hollow PASS on incomplete context — the exact failure mode the ticket aims to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop AC4 entirely for this ticket. If a future criterion genuinely cannot run without children and its absence is provably irrelevant (e.g. a container-only check on a leaf), handle it via the existing 'applies_at' / `applies()` routing in the registry — which already has a tested mechanism for 'this criterion does not apply to this ticket shape' — rather than a new proof/exemption mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` (`_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` and `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`) as consumers that construct `PlanContext` directly and call `finalize_verdict`; specify how each recovery path handles the new completeness/incomplete-context signal.
evidence: gate_dispatch.py:330: `pctx = PlanContext(ticket_id=..., ticket_type=..., title='', description='')` — no completeness flag
gate_dispatch.py:336: `orchestrator.finalize_verdict(pctx, parts, ...)` — consumes the same coverage shape the plan proposes to extend
gate_dispatch.py:351: second bare PlanContext construction in `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`

Plan AC: 'Plan context records whether child enumeration and child detail loading completed successfully' — any new field on PlanContext must be defaulted or set in all construction sites, including these recovery paths outside plan_review/
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` constructs bare `PlanContext` objects directly (lines ~330 and ~370, in `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` and `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure`) and calls `finalize_verdict`, but is not enumerated as a consumer that may require updating.
findings.location: Plan section: Remediation / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: If a `children_complete: bool` (or similar) field is added to `PlanContext`, the two recovery-path constructions in gate_dispatch.py omit it, potentially defaulting to a wrong completeness assumption and bypassing the new INDETERMINATE guard.

If `finalize_verdict` inspects a new `coverage.hierarchy_incomplete` key to decide INDETERMINATE, the recovery paths that build coverage from precheck/decide step outputs won't set it, silently bypassing the new gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `children_complete: bool = True` field (with a safe default) to `PlanContext`, document that recovery-path constructions intentionally default to `True` (they have no hierarchy reads to fail), and verify `finalize_verdict` only checks the flag when the field was explicitly set to `False` by the assembly path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify AC4's relevance-exception mechanism vs. always returning INDETERMINATE on incomplete hierarchy (the simpler established pattern), or remove AC4 and rely solely on AC3 (no ordinary clean pass with incomplete hierarchy).
evidence: AC4: 'The only exception is a deterministic, tested proof that the omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided.'
The existing finalize_verdict already has: `elif coverage.get('llm_unavailable'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` — a clean, no-exception INDETERMINATE for a systemic tier failure.
No example of when missing child context would be demonstrably irrelevant is given; the plan acknowledges P5/P8 are degraded by missing children (the Evidence section).
Search for 'relevance_exception', 'deterministic proof' in the codebase returns no matches — this is a wholly new mechanism.
findings.finding: AC4 introduces a novel 'deterministic, tested proof that omitted context is irrelevant to every criterion being decided' exception path. This is a non-trivial architectural mechanism with no prior-art consideration or justification vs. the simpler established alternative: always return INDETERMINATE on incomplete hierarchy (which is how the system already handles the analogous llm_unavailable case). The plan doesn't cite why the simpler approach is insufficient nor demonstrate that this exception path will be exercised in practice (Rule-of-Three violation).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 / Remediation section ('deterministically prove the missing context is irrelevant')
scenarios: Implementors build an elaborate criterion-exclusion matrix to qualify the exception, adding ongoing maintenance burden for a path that never fires in practice.
The exception is invoked incorrectly for a leaf ticket (no children ever) where the missing-context concern doesn't apply, masking a genuine enumeration failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g., `pytest tests/llm/plan_review/test_orchestrator.py -k 'incomplete_hierarchy'`) to each AC, or group them under a single `pytest` invocation that covers all named scenarios.
evidence: AC1: 'Plan context records whether child enumeration and child detail loading completed successfully.' — no pytest/grep command cited.
AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.' — no proving command.
AC3: 'A remaining incomplete hierarchy cannot receive an ordinary clean pass.' — no command to demonstrate the gate fires.
AC6: 'Tests cover total child-list failure...' — mentions tests exist but gives no run command or file path.
ABSENCE: No single AC contains a command of the form `pytest ...`, `python -m ...`, or structured assertion.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command; every criterion lacks a test invocation or assertion command that would produce pass/fail evidence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 6 ACs)
scenarios: A reviewer cannot verify done-ness without running unspecified tests by convention rather than by plan.
An agent completing this work has no deterministic signal that AC3's gate actually fires on an incomplete hierarchy.
findings.suggested_fix: Append a 'Verification' section: `pytest tests/llm/plan_review/test_orchestrator.py` covering the six scenario classes named in AC6, plus `grep -E '"hierarchy_complete"\s*:' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` to confirm the completeness flag is recorded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default retry count and backoff in the plan (e.g., 'retry up to 3 times with 0.5 s exponential backoff') so the executor can act without choosing an arbitrary value.
evidence: 'bounded retries' appears in both the Remediation and the cascade flag with no count or backoff specified.
ABSENCE: No default retry count (2? 3?) or delay/backoff strategy is provided.
findings.finding: 'Bounded retries' lacks a stated count/backoff, leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary retry policy.
findings.location: AC2 and Remediation: 'Retry recoverable reads. … preserve bounded retries'
scenarios: Two implementers may choose different retry counts, making the behavior non-deterministic across deployments.
Tests written for 3 retries will fail if the implementation uses 2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Default: 3 attempts, 0.5 s initial delay, exponential backoff, total cap 4 s.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing for the behavioral change from 'degrade-and-pass' to 'indeterminate-and-block', covering how to revert if the stricter mode causes unacceptable claim-gate disruption.
evidence: 'tightening failure behavior can affect claim-gate availability during transient tracker failures; preserve bounded retries and actionable diagnostics' — this is a warning, not a rollback plan
Remediation: 'return an explicit incomplete-context/indeterminate result and prohibit an ordinary clean pass' — behavioral breaking change with no stated reversibility mechanism
No expand-contract sequence described for consumers that currently depend on degraded-but-passing behavior
findings.finding: The plan changes orchestrator behavior from 'silently degrade and allow clean pass' to 'block with indeterminate result on incomplete hierarchy', which is a breaking behavioral change for consumers relying on prior graceful degradation. The Cascade flag mentions the impact ('can affect claim-gate availability during transient tracker failures') but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient under T4.
findings.location: Cascade flag / Remediation section
scenarios: A transient tracker outage causes all in-flight plan reviews to return indeterminate, blocking claim gates with no ability to fall back to prior behavior or roll back the change
A deployment of the new behavior needs to be reverted because indeterminate results block CI; no back-out path is documented
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback / expand-contract' subsection: (1) deploy the completeness flag and indeterminate result type behind a feature flag defaulting to 'off'; (2) validate that bounded retries reduce indeterminate rate to acceptable baseline; (3) enable flag; (4) document the flag-flip as the revert path. Remove the flag after one release cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or design-doc section capturing the new 'indeterminate' verdict state, the relevance-exception contract, and the downstream claim-gate handling protocol.
evidence: Cascade flag: 'tightening failure behavior can affect claim-gate availability during transient tracker failures'
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update appears anywhere in the plan
New verdict state 'indeterminate' and the 'relevance exception' mechanism are novel contracts requiring documented decisions for future maintainers
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'indeterminate/incomplete-context' verdict state and a 'relevance exception' contract that cross the orchestrator→verdict→claim-gate boundary, but no ADR or design-doc update is specified to capture this architectural decision and its downstream handling protocol.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria — no mention of ADR or design doc
scenarios: A future engineer modifies the claim-gate without knowing the indeterminate verdict contract exists
The relevance-exception logic is misapplied or silently dropped during refactor because its rationale is undocumented
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or AGENTS.md entry) documenting: (1) when an indeterminate verdict is emitted, (2) how claim gates must treat it, (3) the conditions and required proof for the relevance exception, and (4) the bounded-retry policy for tracker reads.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each new failure point (child-list failure, child-fetch failure, retry exhaustion, indeterminate verdict emission) emits a structured log event and/or increments a named metric/counter so operators can observe and alert on degraded coverage.
evidence: AC bullet 2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics' — 'diagnostics' is unspecified (log? metric? alert?)
AC bullet 5: 'Explicit incomplete-context/indeterminate outcomes identify which hierarchy reads failed' — output format and observability channel not specified
No mention of structured log events, counters, or alerts for retry exhaustion or indeterminate verdict emission
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'actionable diagnostics' for retry failures but does not require any metric, log event, trace, or alert for the new failure points (child enumeration failure, retry exhaustion, indeterminate verdict emission), leaving operators unable to observe when review coverage is degraded.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 2 ('actionable diagnostics') and AC bullet 5 ('identify which hierarchy reads failed')
scenarios: Child enumeration silently fails repeatedly in production; the system emits indeterminate verdicts but no alert fires and no metric spikes
An operator cannot distinguish a one-off transient failure from a systematic tracker outage because no counter or alert exists
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Each child enumeration failure, retry exhaustion, and indeterminate verdict emission emits a structured log entry (including which plan and which child reads failed) and increments a named metric (e.g., plan_review.hierarchy_incomplete_total) observable in the existing monitoring stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-read timeout (and optionally a circuit-breaker) to tracker child-list and child-fetch calls so that slow responses are bounded independently of the retry count.
evidence: AC bullet 2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics' — no timeout or deadline specified
Cascade flag notes claim-gate availability is at risk during 'transient tracker failures' but slow responses are a distinct failure mode not covered by retry count alone
No mention of per-read timeout, overall deadline, or circuit-breaker for the tracker dependency
findings.finding: The plan requires 'bounded retries' for recoverable failures but does not address slow/hung tracker reads — a tracker that responds slowly rather than failing fast can hold a review open indefinitely beyond the retry count if no per-attempt timeout or circuit-breaker is specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 2 / Remediation — 'Retry recoverable reads'
scenarios: Tracker is slow (e.g., 30 s per response) during high load; bounded retries each block for 30 s, holding the review thread for minutes and starving other reviews
Unrelated plan reviews queue behind a stuck tracker read, causing broader availability impact
findings.suggested_fix: Specify a per-attempt read timeout (e.g., configurable, defaulting to a small value like 5 s) and wrap calls in a circuit-breaker or overall deadline so that a slow tracker triggers the same indeterminate-verdict path as a fast failure after the deadline is exceeded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale (1–2 sentences) in the Remediation section explaining why the flag-propagation / verdict-protocol approach was chosen (e.g. alignment with the existing PlanContext/finalize_verdict seam, avoiding new DET-floor coupling).
evidence: No sentence in the Remediation or Finding sections explains why PlanContext/verdict-protocol propagation was preferred over alternatives
The cascade flag mentions preserving bounded retries and diagnostics but does not explain the architectural choice
findings.finding: The plan states the chosen approach ('Make hierarchy completeness explicit in the review context and verdict protocol') but provides no positive rationale for WHY this approach was selected — e.g. why flag-propagation through PlanContext rather than a new DET-floor check or a raise-at-assembly strategy. The absence of a positive rationale leaves the implementer without grounding for trade-off decisions during implementation.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'Make hierarchy completeness explicit in the review context and verdict protocol. Retry recoverable reads.'
scenarios: An implementer considers adding a new DET P-check instead, not knowing the plan author considered and rejected this; the resulting implementation diverges from the intended design

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `_reads.list_tickets` / `_reads.show_ticket` are safe to call multiple times in a retry loop (idempotency of `_fresh(tracker)`), and explicitly scope where the retry layer lives — either at the call site in `_assemble_context_uncached` or in a new wrapper — before committing to bounded retries in AC2.
evidence: AC2: 'Recoverable list/fetch failures receive bounded retries with actionable diagnostics.'

_reads.show_ticket and _reads.list_tickets both call _fresh(tracker) internally, which is a write-side freshness operation — the plan does not verify that this is safe to invoke multiple times in a retry loop.
No retry layer exists anywhere in src/rebar/_reads.py, src/rebar/_lib_reads.py, or src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'bounded retries with actionable diagnostics' for list_tickets / show_ticket failures, but these are synchronous library calls (_reads.py) with no existing retry infrastructure. The plan makes no assertion about whether the reads are idempotent under transient failure, where the retry layer would live, or what 'bounded' means (count, backoff). If the underlying store is not idempotently retryable (e.g., it writes _fresh markers), retries could corrupt state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 / src/rebar/_reads.py show_ticket + list_tickets
scenarios: A retry loop wrapping _reads.show_ticket calls _fresh(tracker) N+1 times instead of once, causing unexpected tracker state.
A non-transient error (e.g., malformed ticket id) is retried N times before failing, delaying the review and degrading UX with misleading 'retrying' diagnostics.
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect `_fresh(tracker)` semantics. If it is idempotent (pure read-freshness check, no write), document this in the plan and locate the retry wrapper at the `_assemble_context_uncached` call sites. If not idempotent, remove the retry AC and replace with a single-attempt diagnostic (the exception is already caught and logged; strengthen the log message instead).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
